bootstrap.author: redeyeflyer
bootstrap.title: US airports ranked by 'I would actually eat here on purpose', from someone who lives in seat 34B
bootstrap.body:
Top tier: anywhere in the Southwest with a real green chile situation, and Portland, because PDX somehow convinced actual local restaurants to move in. Honourable mention to any Texas airport where the barbecue is genuinely from the barbecue place and not 'inspired by' it.

Bottom tier: the sad refrigerated sandwich case at whichever regional terminal I was in last week. The sandwich had been folded, somehow. The whole sandwich.

What's the layover food you'd actually route through on purpose?

comments.author: flight_mode
comments.body: PDX is the correct answer and it's not close. Real local food, carpet with a fan club, and free water fountains that actually work. I've taken a longer connection through there on purpose and I regret nothing.

replies.author: redeyeflyer
replies.body: Booking the longer layover for the food is the frequent flyer version of taking the scenic route. Respect.

comments.author: wanderlust_on_layaway
comments.body: budget traveller take: the real move is knowing which airports have a grocery store on the transit line in. I've picnicked in more US terminals than I've dined in. Folded sandwich case never sees a dime from me

comments.author: maplebound
comments.body: As a Canadian I'm mostly just impressed by the scale of it all. I once had a connection in Atlanta and I'm fairly sure the walk between gates crossed a time zone. The sweet tea at the halfway point was medically necessary.

bootstrap.author: wanderlust_on_layaway
bootstrap.title: Drove the Blue Ridge Parkway on a shoestring and it beat every big-ticket trip I've ever saved for
bootstrap.body:
No entrance fee for the parkway itself. 469 miles of ridgeline from Virginia down into North Carolina, overlooks every few minutes, and the speed limit basically forces you to relax.

We camped cheap, bought groceries in the little towns off the exits, and split gas two ways. Total cost was less than one theme park day for a family. Diner biscuits and gravy in some tiny spot outside Asheville was the best meal of the trip and it cost single digits.

America does free scenery better than anywhere I've been, you just have to drive to it.

comments.author: redeyeflyer
comments.body: The parkway is the rare road where the journey actually is the destination and not just a thing people stitch on pillows. Pro tip for anyone going: fill the tank before you get on, services on the parkway itself are sparse on purpose.

comments.author: trailmixtake
comments.body: Adding the trail note: dozens of the overlooks have short hikes right off the parking area, so you can turn a scenic drive into a sampler platter of little summits. I ranked my top five in a notebook like a dork. Craggy Gardens won.

replies.author: wanderlust_on_layaway
replies.body: We did Craggy Gardens on your kind of whim and it was the best free hour of the trip. The notebook system works.

comments.author: peaks_and_pierogies
comments.body: The Blue Ridge is what I recommend to Europeans who think American scenery is only the giant western stuff. Old soft mountains, small towns, church potluck energy. It's the Carpathians with better road maintenance.

bootstrap.author: umami_bomb
bootstrap.title: Regional BBQ styles, judged by a food-science pedant who loves them all unequally
bootstrap.body:
Texas brisket: salt, pepper, smoke, patience. The confidence of a style that seasons with two things and dares you to complain.

Carolina whole hog with vinegar sauce: scientifically correct. The acid cuts the fat exactly where your palate needs it. Eastern NC folks have known this longer than food science has had a name for it.

Memphis dry rub ribs: proof that a crust can be a sauce. Kansas City: sweet, thick, unapologetic, the style most likely to end up on your shirt and your fries.

They're all valid ecosystems. But if I'm picking one last plate, it's vinegar sauce and I'm ready for the consequences of saying so.

comments.author: spice_cabinet
comments.body: My measurements are 'some' so the Texas approach of two seasonings applied with conviction speaks to my soul. My mother would call the brisket underseasoned, then finish her plate, then ask for the technique while denying she asked.

replies.author: umami_bomb
replies.body: Finishing the plate while disputing the seasoning is the highest compliment in any food culture. Universal grandmother behaviour.

comments.author: salt_beef_bucket
comments.body: Newfoundlander here so my smoked meat experience is mostly cod, but vinegar cutting fat is just science we've always done with mustard pickles and a Jiggs dinner. The Carolinas would understand us.

comments.author: redeyeflyer
comments.body: I've eaten all four styles within one work quarter, which my doctor calls a warning sign and I call field research. Verdict: whichever one is closest to the airport at the time. But vinegar sauce travels best in memory.

drip.author: flight_mode
drip.title: Did three US national parks with one carry-on: what earned its place and what flew home in shame
drip.body:
Earned it: merino everything, one packable down layer even in July because desert nights don't care about the season, and a collapsible water bottle that took up zero space.

Flew home in shame: the second pair of shoes, the 'just in case' rain shell in Utah, and a paperback I somehow never opened because the scenery kept happening.

Zion, Bryce, and the Grand Canyon north rim, all on one bag. The gear list gets shorter every trip and the trips keep getting better. Related? Related.

comments.author: redeyeflyer
comments.body: The 'just in case' item is always the one that flies home unused. Always. My personal shame item was a laptop I carried through four states and opened once, in an airport, to check in for the flight home.

comments.author: wanderlust_on_layaway
comments.body: The one-bag life is also the budget life: no checked bag fees means more money for park entrance passes. The annual America the Beautiful pass pays for itself by park three, for anyone doing this math at home.

replies.author: flight_mode
replies.body: Can confirm the pass math. Park three was Bryce and it felt like walking in free. Best 80 dollars in American travel.

drip.author: maplebound
drip.title: Canadian in an American grocery store: the cereal aisle alone deserves its own postal code
drip.body:
Crossed over for a paddling trip and did the traditional pilgrimage to a giant US grocery store. An entire aisle of cereal. A cheese section with its own weather system. Seventeen kinds of ranch dressing, and I checked, because at some point it became a census.

Came home with the classics: the cheaper gas station snacks, the weird sodas we can't get, and the humbling knowledge that our milk comes in bags and theirs comes in jugs the size of a canoe barrel. Neither system is wrong. Mine is just funnier.

comments.author: umami_bomb
comments.body: The ranch census is real and undercounted. Fun fact you can verify on any label: half those ranches differ mainly in buttermilk ratio and garlic placement. Americans have speciated a single dressing into an ecosystem and honestly, respect.

comments.author: salt_beef_bucket
comments.body: Every Newfoundlander who visits the States comes home with a suitcase of snacks and a story about the portion sizes. My cousin ordered a 'medium' drink and needed both hands. We still talk about it at Christmas.

late_comments.author: trailmixtake
late_comments.body: Late addition for the rankings: any airport within striking distance of a mountain town counts the trailhead as terminal food in my books. I've eaten a summit sandwich on a layover. Different sport, same league.

late_comments.author: marinara_line
late_comments.body: Italian food heritage person wandering in to say the vinegar people are right and this is exactly how we feel about acid in ragù. Fat needs an argument. The Carolinas understood the assignment.

late_comments.author: flight_mode
late_comments.body: Filing this under trips that don't need a checked bag or a second mortgage. The best American itineraries are the ones where the scenery is the entertainment budget.
